summary: DHH shares a brief, humorous observation about instant messenger away bots talking to each other. He compares IM auto-responses to old-fashioned butlers, noting how they dutifully inform visitors when their master is unavailable. The post captures a moment where two away-bots end up in a comical exchange, each telling the other that their human is gone. It's a lighthearted early-blog-era vignette about the unintended humor of automated systems.
key_points: Instant messenger auto-responses function like digital butlers, handling social obligations on behalf of absent users
Automated systems can produce unintentionally humorous interactions when they talk to each other
Early internet culture found charm in the quirks of rudimentary automation
money_quotes: The instant messenger has come butler of yester-century.
Today, I even caught mine chatting on my behalf with a like-minded fellow belonging to a friend.
key_insight: Even the simplest automated systems can produce unexpectedly charming behavior when they interact with each other without human oversight.
